column1: #deck:Terraform AWS Mastery::Phase 1 - Foundations::05-variables-locals-outputs
#separator:Comma
#columns:Front
column2: Back

column1: A variable is marked sensitive = true. Is its value encrypted or protected in terraform.tfstate?
column2: No. sensitive = true ONLY redacts the value from terminal output (plan/apply logs, terraform output display). The value is written to terraform.tfstate in plaintext, exactly like any other value. State security requires storing state securely (encrypted S3 backend, IAM access control) — not the sensitive flag.

column1: What is the difference between sensitive = true and ephemeral = true on a variable?
column2: sensitive = true: value is redacted from terminal output but IS written to state and plan files. ephemeral = true: value exists only in memory during plan/apply — NEVER written to state, plan files, or logs. Ephemeral values can only flow into ephemeral outputs (child modules only) or write-only resource arguments.

column1: What are the two valid ephemeral contexts for an ephemeral variable value?
column2: (1) An ephemeral = true output block in a child module (NOT a root module — ephemeral outputs are invalid in root modules and error at validate). (2) A write_only resource argument (Terraform 1.10+, covered in Demo 08). Regular resource arguments are NOT ephemeral contexts.

column1: What does nullable = false on a variable do?
column2: If null is passed as the variable value, the default is used instead. By default (nullable = true), explicitly passing null overrides the variable to null — even if a default exists. nullable = false prevents this: null input → default is used.

column1: A validation block's condition returns the string 'dev' instead of a boolean. What happens?
column2: terraform validate errors: 'A condition expression must return either true or false.' The condition must be a boolean expression — use contains(), can(), regex(), or comparison operators. A string is not valid.

column1: Inside a validation block, what is the ONLY variable reference allowed in the condition?
column2: Only var.<this_variable> — the variable being validated. You cannot reference other variables, locals, resources, or data sources. Attempting to do so errors with 'Invalid reference.'

column1: State the variable value precedence order from highest to lowest.
column2: 1. CLI -var flag, 2. CLI -var-file flag, 3. *.auto.tfvars files (alphabetical), 4. terraform.tfvars, 5. TF_VAR_ environment variables, 6. default value in the variable block, 7. interactive prompt (avoided in CI).

column1: What is the distinction test for variable vs. local?
column2: If you would ever want to override this value from outside the configuration (different per environment, engineer, or run) — it is a variable. If it is always derived from other values in the configuration and never needs external input — it is a local. Locals earn their place through transformation, not pass-through.

column1: What are three key differences between variables and locals?
column2: (1) Variables can be set from outside (CLI, env, tfvars); locals cannot. (2) Variables have type/description/sensitive/nullable/validation arguments; locals have none of these. (3) Locals can reference resources and data sources; variables cannot (only var.<this_variable> in validation).

column1: Do locals have a type argument? How is a local's type determined?
column2: No type argument exists for locals. Terraform infers the type from the assigned expression. A locals block with all string values is inferred as map(string). Mixed value types produce object({...}). You cannot declare a type constraint on a local.

column1: What type does Terraform infer for a local defined as { Project = var.project, Environment = var.environment } when both variables are type = string?
column2: map(string) — all values are strings. If any value were a different type (e.g. a number), Terraform would infer object({...}) instead.

column1: What is the difference between list(string), set(string), and map(string)?
column2: list: ordered, allows duplicates, indexed by position [0]. set: unordered, no duplicates, no positional index — sets have no literal syntax, use toset([...]). map: key-value pairs, keys are strings, no guaranteed order. All three require all elements to be the same type.

column1: What does can(regex('pattern', var.x)) return when the pattern does not match?
column2: false — regex() errors when there is no match, and can() converts any error into false. This is the idiomatic pattern for regex-based validation: can(regex(...)) returns true on match, false on no match.

column1: What does alltrue([]) return?
column2: true — alltrue() returns true if ALL elements are true. An empty list satisfies this vacuously (no false elements). This is why validation conditions using alltrue([for item in var.list : ...]) pass when the list is empty.

column1: What does coalesce(null, '', 'first-real', 'second') return?
column2: 'first-real' — coalesce() skips both null AND empty string (''), returning the first value that is neither. Empty string is treated the same as null.

column1: What is the difference between try() and coalesce()?
column2: try(expr, fallback) catches ERRORS — returns the first argument that evaluates without error. coalesce(val1, val2) skips null and empty string — returns the first non-null, non-empty value. Use try() for optional object attributes or failing type conversions. Use coalesce() for 'use this value if set, otherwise fall back.'

column1: You call merge(local.common_tags, var.extra_tags). Both have key 'Owner'. Which value appears?
column2: var.extra_tags's value — merge() uses right-most-wins for key conflicts. merge(common, extra) means extra wins. merge(extra, common) would mean common wins.

column1: Can locals reference other locals? Does the order they are written in matter?
column2: Yes, locals can reference other locals. No, order does not matter — Terraform resolves the dependency order automatically from references within each expression, exactly as it does for resources.

column1: What happens if two locals reference each other circularly?
column2: terraform plan errors: 'Cycle in local values: local.a -> local.b -> local.a'. Detected at plan time before evaluating any value. Fix: break the cycle by extracting the shared value into a third local that neither side references back.

column1: What does data.aws_caller_identity return and what arguments does it require?
column2: No arguments — body is empty ({}). Makes a single sts:GetCallerIdentity API call. Returns: account_id (AWS account ID), arn (ARN of the caller), user_id (unique caller ID).

column1: What is the difference between aws_iam_role_policy (inline) and aws_iam_policy (managed)?
column2: Inline: attached to exactly one role, destroyed with the role, cannot be reused. Managed: standalone resource attachable to multiple roles via aws_iam_role_policy_attachment. Use inline for role-specific permissions, managed for shared permissions.

column1: terraform output role_unique_id shows (sensitive value). Does terraform output -json also redact it?
column2: No — terraform output -json always shows the actual value even for sensitive outputs. -json is for programmatic consumption. Treat -json output with the same care as the state file.

column1: Can terraform_remote_state read sensitive = true outputs from the producer?
column2: Yes — sensitive = true only redacts from terminal display. The value is in the state file in plaintext and any consumer with s3:GetObject access can read it via terraform_remote_state. All non-ephemeral outputs are accessible regardless of sensitivity.

column1: Can terraform_remote_state read ephemeral = true outputs from the producer?
column2: No — ephemeral outputs are never written to state. Since terraform_remote_state reads the state file, ephemeral outputs simply do not exist there.

column1: What IAM permissions does the consumer need for terraform_remote_state with S3 backend?
column2: s3:GetObject on the state bucket and key path, using the consumer's own AWS credentials from the consumer's provider block. No special Terraform permissions — it is a standard S3 read.

column1: A producer has never been applied. What happens when the consumer runs terraform plan with terraform_remote_state?
column2: plan fails immediately: 'Error loading state error: NoSuchKey — the specified key does not exist.' Handle by applying the producer first, or wrapping the reference in try() with a fallback for bootstrap scenarios.

column1: You run terraform plan -var='environment=prod' with TF_VAR_environment=staging also set. Which value wins?
column2: prod — CLI -var flag has higher precedence than TF_VAR_ environment variables. Full order: CLI -var > CLI -var-file > *.auto.tfvars > terraform.tfvars > TF_VAR_ > default.

column1: What does terraform output -raw do differently from terraform output <NAME>?
column2: terraform output <NAME> prints the value with surrounding quotes and formatting. terraform output -raw <NAME> prints the raw value with no quotes or extra characters — suitable for capturing in a shell variable: ROLE_ARN=$(terraform output -raw role_arn).

column1: What does terraform output -no-color do and when is it needed?
column2: Strips ANSI colour escape codes from output. Without it, CI log systems that do not interpret ANSI display literal escape sequences (e.g. ESC[32m) instead of colour formatting. Use in any CI environment where output is captured as plain text.

column1: Is ephemeral = true on an output valid in a root module?
column2: No. Ephemeral outputs are only valid in child modules. terraform validate errors in a root module: 'Ephemeral output not allowed — Ephemeral outputs are not allowed in context of a root module.' Root modules have no caller to receive the ephemeral value.

column1: What is the conditional operator syntax in Terraform, and why is it the only conditional mechanism?
column2: Syntax: condition ? value_if_true : value_if_false. HCL has no if statement — the conditional expression IS the if/else mechanism. For conditional resource creation: count = var.create ? 1 : 0.

column1: What does coalesce() return when called with coalesce('', 'fallback')?
column2: 'fallback' — coalesce() treats empty string the same as null, skipping both. Only non-null, non-empty string values satisfy coalesce.
